text: Since the release of ChatGPT, there has been considerable debate in academia regarding the implications of large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-3.5 for academic work. On the one hand, these models can facilitate or even enhance scholarly work; on the other hand, they also carry a number of ethical risks, for example, concerning academic plagiarism, the dissemination of fake news, or environmental damage during model training. As there is not yet an adequate amount of formal research in this regard, despite the abundance of “hacks” published online about the use of AI and smart tools in research, the present paper reports on an “experience experiment”. Using the text-davinci-003 model, the author of this paper wrote an entire academic research paper, which is explored within five different collaborative workflows or “modes” suggested by the application such as Chunk Stylist, Bullet-to-Paragraph, Talk Textualizer, Research Buddy, and finally Polisher. It describes their effect on productivity, motivation and authorship and concludes with some possible references on academic plagiarism to safeguard ethical authorship standards. In order to demonstrate the kind of transparency as it relates to authorship and text flow suggested by the AI-assisted collaborative workflows applied in this experience-experiment, all input prompts provided to the model, the outputs given by the AI application as well as all subsequent human-made amendments have been documented along with the corresponding datasets showing the percentages of the numbers of replaced words and phrases in between the outputs generated by the application and the revised texts provided by human scholars and authors.
